chunk_summary: This section discusses dietary modifications for transient lactose intolerance, cow’s milk protein allergy, and celiac disease, emphasizing proper diagnosis, management, and nutritional balance to prevent complications.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between transient lactose intolerance and cow’s milk protein allergy based on clinical features.
Understand the dietary management of secondary lactose intolerance and celiac disease.
Recognize red flags requiring referral in cases of chronic gastrointestinal symptoms.
Learn the importance of avoiding unnecessary dietary restrictions and ensuring balanced nutrition.
key_facts: Secondary lactose intolerance often follows mucosal injury and presents with watery stools, bloating, and perianal rash after milk consumption.
Management of lactose intolerance includes temporary lactose reduction and reintroduction as symptoms improve to avoid prolonged diet narrowing.
Cow’s milk protein allergy is suggested by blood or mucus in stools, eczema, wheeze, vomiting, or poor growth after milk exposure.
Celiac disease should be suspected in cases of chronic diarrhea, abdominal distension, anemia, or persistent undernutrition despite adequate intake.
Gluten-free diets should not be started before testing for celiac disease to avoid false-negative serology results.
Referral is recommended for red flags such as blood in stool, failure to thrive, dehydration, or nocturnal symptoms.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Secondary lactose intolerance typically follows mucosal injury and presents with watery stools, abdominal bloating, and perianal rash after milk, without blood in stool or systemic allergy features. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532285/)
document_key_topics: Celiac disease and gluten-free diet guidelines
